title: Apply: photosynthesis in organisms
description: Apply your understanding of photosynthesis in organisms in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.

assessment_items.question_content:
During and after photosynthesis, energy changes from one form to another in a series of steps.

**Which step related to this energy transformation occurs *first*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Light energy is used to build sugars from carbon dioxide and water.
Sugar molecules are rearranged, releasing chemical energy.

assessment_items.question_content:
During and after photosynthesis, energy changes from one form to another in a series of steps.

**Which step related to this energy transformation occurs *second*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Light energy is used to build sugars from carbon dioxide and water.
Sugar molecules are rearranged, releasing chemical energy.

assessment_items.question_content:
During and after photosynthesis, energy changes from one form to another in a series of steps.

**Which step related to this energy transformation occurs *last*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Light energy is used to build sugars from carbon dioxide and water.
Sugar molecules are rearranged, releasing chemical energy.

assessment_items.question_content:
Three groups of students carried out an experiment about photosynthesis. First, the students submerged *Elodea* plants in a beaker of water mixed with baking soda (Figure 1). Then, they placed the beaker directly under a lamp.

[[☃ image 1]]

With the lamp off, the students counted how many bubbles they saw rise from the plants. After three minutes of observations, each group recorded their results. Next, the students turned on the lamp. For three minutes, they again counted and recorded the number of bubbles they saw rise from the plants.

This data table shows the results of the students’ experiment:

  | Number of bubbles (light off) | Number of bubbles (light on)
- | :-: | :-:
Group 1 |2 | 175
Group 2 | 5 | 192
Group 3 | 3 | 210

**Which statement best explains the results of the experiment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Elodea plants are shown under water in a beaker.
options.caption: Figure 1. *Elodea* plants submerged in a beaker of water mixed with baking soda.

choices.content: Light increased the rate of photosynthesis in the plants, causing more bubbles of oxygen to be released.
Light increased the rate of photosynthesis in the plants, causing fewer bubbles of oxygen to be released.
Light decreased the rate of photosynthesis in the plants, causing more bubbles of carbon dioxide to be released.

assessment_items.question_content:
Three groups of students carried out an experiment about photosynthesis. First, the students submerged *Elodea* plants in a beaker of water mixed with baking soda (Figure 1). Then, they placed the beaker directly under a lamp.

[[☃ image 1]]

With the lamp off, the students counted how many bubbles they saw rise from the plants. After three minutes of observations, each group recorded their results. Next, the students turned on the lamp. For three minutes, they again counted and recorded the number of bubbles they saw rise from the plants.

**Based on what you know about photosynthesis, which data table shows the most likely results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Elodea plants are shown under water in a beaker.
options.caption: Figure 1. *Elodea* plants submerged in a beaker of water mixed with baking soda.

choices.content:
| Number of bubbles (light off) | Number of bubbles (light on)
- | :-: | :-:
Group 1 |2 | 175
Group 2 | 5 | 192
Group 3 | 3 | 210

| Number of bubbles (light off) | Number of bubbles (light on)
- | :-: | :-:
Group 1 |175 | 2
Group 2 | 192 | 5
Group 3 | 210 | 3

| Number of bubbles (light off) | Number of bubbles (light on)
- | :-: | :-:
Group 1 |182 | 175
Group 2 | 193 | 192
Group 3 | 215 | 210

assessment_items.question_content:
Three groups of students are going to carry out an experiment about photosynthesis. First, the students will submerge *Elodea* plants in a beaker of water mixed with baking soda (Figure 1). Then, they will place the beaker directly under a lamp.

[[☃ image 1]]

With the lamp off, the students will count how many bubbles they see rise from the plants. After three minutes of observations, each group will record their results. Next, the students will turn on the lamp. For three minutes, they will again count and record the number of bubbles they see rise from the plants.

**Based on what you know about photosynthesis, what do you predict will happen during the experiment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Elodea plants are shown under water in a beaker.
options.caption: Figure 1. *Elodea* plants submerged in a beaker of water mixed with baking soda.

choices.content: More bubbles will be released when the light is on compared to when the light is off.
Fewer bubbles will be released when the light is on compared to when the light is off.
About the same number of bubbles will be released whether the light is on or off.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram has arrows representing inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which arrow best represents the movement of carbon dioxide during photosynthesis?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A diagram represents the process of photosynthesis. The top left corner of the diagram shows a sun. There are light waves pointing from the sun to a green plant in the center of the diagram. An arrow labeled A points from the air to the green parts of the plant. An arrow labeled B points from the soil to the plant’s roots. An arrow labeled C points from the green parts of the plant to the air.
